candidate_problems.text: What operational criterion could distinguish 'corrigible explanation' from merely fluent recombination in token-predicting systems? The conjecture shifts the boundary from mechanism to error-correctability, but leaves open how to test whether an output genuinely functions as a conjecture that can survive criticism, guide intervention, and improve under correction rather than only mimic those properties.

What system-level structures are required for token prediction to participate in genuine knowledge growth rather than isolated one-off performance? The conjecture implies that knowledge creation depends not just on output quality but on feedback loops, memory, revision, and exposure to consequences; the unresolved question is which architectural and social arrangements make those loops real and productive.

Can explanatory novelty be defined without requiring independence from training data, while still avoiding the collapse of 'knowledge' into any useful recombination? The conjecture rejects ex nihilo originality, but it remains open how to characterize novelty, abstraction, and problem-solving consequences in a way that preserves a substantive notion of knowledge rather than redescribing all successful pattern extension as knowledge creation.
